w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh w16sdtfl w16du wp14

w:r.w:t: Automatic Cost Updates on Invoice Confirmation
Decision A — Popularity Data Source for Menu Engineering
Decision B — Free Tier Plan Gate Behaviour

is a multi-tenant SaaS platform designed to give restaurant owners and food-truck operators complete visibility into their food costs, margins, and purchasing decisions. The system is built as a fully containerised, production-ready application from day one, with a Go API backend, a Next.js 16 frontend, PostgreSQL as the primary data store, Redis for caching and job queuing, and
Food cost management is one of the highest-leverage activities in any food-service business. A 1% reduction in food cost percentage on a $1M revenue operation saves $10,000 annually.
gives operators the data layer to achieve and maintain that improvement by:
Calculating the exact cost of every recipe and menu item in real time.
Tracking inventory with full movement history and theoretical vs. actual variance.
Surfacing pricing suggestions and menu engineering insights.
Managing supplier relationships and purchase orders in one place.
Delivering detailed analytics with CSV export and trend history.
Single responsibility per service — no business logic in the proxy, no DB calls in the frontend.
Tenant isolation at the database layer via PostgreSQL Row-Level Security — not just application checks.
All blocking I/O (email, webhooks, large exports, CSV processing) is offloaded to the async worker.
One Go binary serves dual roles (API and worker) via a startup flag, keeping the image count minimal.
Redis holds only ephemeral data: active access token sessions, magic link tokens, email verification tokens, password reset tokens, and the
queue broker. All durable auth state (refresh tokens, user records) lives in PostgreSQL.
PostgreSQL is the single source of truth for all persistent state.
Reverse proxy, TLS termination, Docker-based routing
REST API, JWT middleware, domain handlers
Access token sessions, magic link tokens, email/password-reset tokens,
terminates TLS and routes: path prefix /
Go API middleware chain runs: JWT validation → tenant extraction (sets
in Postgres session) → role check → plan gate.
Domain handler executes business logic, calls store layer.
Store layer runs parameterised queries against PostgreSQL. RLS policies enforce tenant isolation transparently.
Response serialised as JSON, returned through
The Go API never constructs dynamic SQL. All queries use
named parameters. Tenant scoping is a belt-and-suspenders arrangement: the JWT claim carries
and the Postgres session variable enforces it at the DB level.
The entire data model is anchored on a four-level ownership hierarchy. Every table below Venue carries both
. PostgreSQL RLS policies enforce tenant isolation: the Go API sets SET LOCAL
= $1 at the start of every transaction, and every policy checks
. No password — auth is magic link or OAuth only.
. A user may belong to multiple orgs.
. Cost fields are computed on read.
), quantity, unit. Supports sub-recipe nesting up to 3 levels.
(FK, nullable — set only for purchase movements), notes,
(FK nullable — linked when invoice is matched).
— actual receipt is driven exclusively by the matched
Authentication supports three mechanisms: email + password (primary), magic links (
), and OAuth (social login). All three converge on the same token issuance pipeline — a short-lived JWT access token plus a long-lived refresh token stored in PostgreSQL.
Access token sessions are stored in Redis (fast lookup, automatic TTL-based expiry). Refresh tokens are stored in PostgreSQL (durable, revocable,
for security audits). These are two distinct stores with two distinct responsibilities.
, exp. Sent as Authorization: Bearer header.
Opaque 32-byte random token. Stored in PostgreSQL
=Strict cookie. Used only to rotate the access token pair.
Created on every login or token refresh. Key:
Every live access token has a matching session record in Redis. This is the authoritative revocation check — a token whose session has been deleted or has
set is immediately invalid even if the JWT signature is still valid.
Session identifier. Stored in the JWT
Session owner. Enables “revoke all sessions for user” queries.
). Compared on every request to detect token substitution.
Client IP at session creation. Used for anomaly detection.
Client user-agent. Enables device listing in the account security page.
True if the user completed MFA in this session. Reserved for future MFA feature.
Sliding expiry refreshed on each authenticated request. Hard ceiling =
Hard session ceiling regardless of activity.
Updated on every authenticated request. Used for idle timeout enforcement.
Set on logout or forced revocation. Non-null = session is immediately dead.
Refresh tokens live in PostgreSQL so they survive Redis restarts, can be listed per device, and can be
Owner. Indexed for list-all-sessions queries and bulk revocation.
). The raw token exists only in the
Links to the Redis session created alongside this refresh token.
30 days from issuance. Hard ceiling — no sliding window for refresh tokens.
Set on logout, password change, or forced revocation. Non-null = dead.
API loads User by email. If not found → return 401 with a generic message (no user enumeration).
API verifies the submitted password against the stored
If account email is not verified → 403 with error code
On success: create Redis session, issue signed 15-min JWT (
row in Postgres, return JWT in response body + refresh token in
email format, password strength (min 8 chars, at least 1 uppercase, 1 digit, 1 special character).
If email already exists → 409 Conflict with generic wording (no enumeration).
dispatched. Worker sends signed verification link (token SHA-256 hashed in Redis, TTL 24h, single-use).
User cannot log in until the verification link is clicked (enforced in the login flow at step 4 above).
API always returns 200 OK regardless of whether the email exists, to prevent enumeration.
If the account exists: generate a cryptographically random 32-byte token, store SHA-256(token) in Redis under key
dispatches the email with the link /auth/
GET /auth/verify: API hashes the incoming token, looks up Redis, validates TTL, deletes the key (
If the User has no password set (magic-link-only account), email is marked verified automatically on first use.
On success: same session + token issuance pipeline as
, state param) stored in a short-lived signed cookie. No Redis involvement.
record. If a User with the same email already exists, the OAuth identity is linked to the existing account.
On success: same session + token issuance pipeline as all other auth methods.
cookie is sent automatically by the browser.
API reads the raw refresh token from the cookie, computes SHA-256, queries
Revokes the old refresh token row (sets
Creates a new Redis session and a new Postgres
Returns new JWT access token in the response body. Sets a new
cookie with the new refresh token. Old cookie is overwritten.
1) revocation check and activity update on every request.
owner | admin | viewer — enforced by role middleware per route.
Issued-at timestamp. Used for audit logs and debugging.
Executed in order on every authenticated request:
Extract Bearer token from Authorization header. Return 401 if absent or malformed.
Verify JWT signature and exp claim. Return 401 if invalid or expired.
for RLS. Inject auth context into Go request context.
checks as required by the route definition.
POST /auth/forgot-password: generate reset token, store SHA-256(token) in Redis (TTL 1h, single-use), dispatch email via
-hash new password, update User, revoke ALL
rows for user, delete ALL Redis sessions for user (force logout all devices).
POST /auth/change-password (authenticated): verify current password first. Then same pipeline as reset. All other sessions revoked.
/min per IP with progressive delay after 5 failures. POST /auth/register — 5
/min per IP. All auth error responses use identical timing and wording to prevent user enumeration. Password reset and email verification tokens are single-use SHA-256 hashed values in Redis.
One user account can belong to multiple organisations, each with independent billing and venue collections. Roles are scoped per-organisation membership.
Full access. Can manage billing, invite/remove members, delete the organisation.
Full operational access. Cannot manage billing or delete the organisation.
Read-only access to all modules. Cannot create, edit, or delete any records.
The active venue is stored in the JWT claim
. The frontend displays a venue switcher in the top navigation. Switching venue triggers a token refresh endpoint that reissues the JWT with the new

from the JWT and checks a static feature flag map before allowing access to restricted endpoints. Free: 1 venue max, 50 recipes max, no analytics export. Pro: 3 venues, unlimited recipes, full analytics. Business: unlimited venues, webhook API, white-label options.
Recipes are the financial centre of the platform. Every recipe has an automatically computed cost derived from its ingredients' current unit prices.
) for all lines, including sub-recipe rollup
Trend charts: food cost % of a recipe over time, even when ingredient prices changed.
Audit trail: which electronic invoice triggered each price change, the invoice date, supplier, and line description. Manual overrides are also tracked (
= manual) but are clearly distinguished from invoice-driven updates.

row can reference either an Ingredient (leaf) or another Recipe (sub-recipe). The cost rollup is recursive up to 3 levels. The Go domain layer detects cycles before saving and rejects circular references with a 422
List all recipes with computed cost, margin, and food cost %.
Create recipe with ingredients. Returns computed cost immediately.
Update recipe or ingredient list. Recomputes cost.
Stateless scaling — returns adjusted quantities and cost. No DB
Returns cost over time using snapshot records.
Inventory is managed as an immutable movement log. The current stock level for any ingredient is always computed as
) across all movements — there is no mutable "current quantity" field. This design makes the inventory fully auditable and supports retroactive corrections.

Purchase stock movements (type = purchase) are created exclusively by the electronic invoice import pipeline. There is no manual "mark as received" button on a PO. Stock is only incremented when a supplier's electronic invoice is uploaded, parsed, matched to ingredients, and confirmed by the user. This guarantees that stock levels and ingredient costs are always backed by a real fiscal document.
Created automatically and exclusively by the electronic invoice import pipeline after the user confirms the ingredient matching. Carries a
Manual entry or future POS integration. Records theoretical consumption of ingredients.
Explicit waste recording. Separated from usage for waste % reporting.
Manual stock count correction. Requires a mandatory notes field for audit. Used for physical inventory counts.
Stock moved between venues within the same organisation.
For venues that record portions sold (manual input or CSV import),
usage + waste movements) for the same period.
Positive variance indicates over-use or unrecorded waste. This is one of the most actionable metrics in the platform.
is written that brings current stock below an ingredient's
. The worker sends an email and, if configured, fires

The Supplier module manages vendor relationships and purchase orders. Purchase orders are optional planning tools — they can be created to track expected deliveries and matched against incoming invoices, but they are not required. Inventory stock and ingredient costs are updated exclusively when a supplier's electronic invoice is uploaded and confirmed.
Editable. Line items, quantities, and expected prices can be set. No inventory or cost effect.
Locked. Supplier has been notified. Awaiting electronic invoice upload.
An electronic invoice has been uploaded and linked to this PO. Awaiting user confirmation of ingredient matching.
User has confirmed ingredient matching. Invoice import pipeline runs: purchase movements created, unit costs updated.
Terminal state. Invoice data, PO lines, and all movements preserved for audit.
Terminal state. No inventory or cost effect. Reason field required.
When an electronic invoice is uploaded, the system attempts to auto-match it to an open PO by supplier and approximate date. If a match is found with high confidence, the PO is advanced to
automatically. If no PO exists, the invoice can be processed standalone — no PO is required for stock and cost updates.
Automatic Cost Updates on Invoice Confirmation
When the user confirms the ingredient matching from an electronic invoice, the Go domain layer:
of type purchase per matched line (
using an Exponentially Weighted Moving Average (EWMA) with α = 0.3 by default (configurable per venue). This smooths price spikes while tracking drift.
changed (used for trend charts and audit trail).
If linked to a PO, advances the PO status to matched and then immediately to closed.

Menu engineering applies the classic BCG-style matrix analysis to menu items, combining profitability (contribution margin) and popularity (portions sold) to classify each item and surface actionable recommendations.
Protect. Feature prominently. Consider slight price increase test.
Investigate cost reduction or price increase. High volume means high impact.
Increase visibility. Reposition on menu. Run promotions.
Evaluate removal. Simplifies operations and improves average margin.
. Venue target defaults to 30% and is configurable.
, returns recalculated food cost %, gross margin, and projected monthly gross profit at a given volume. Stateless endpoint — no DB write.
per menu item. v1 supports manual input and CSV import. A future POS integration endpoint is reserved at POST /venues
field updated by whichever source is active.
All analytics in v1 are computed queries against PostgreSQL. No separate OLAP store is required at v1 scale. Each report endpoint accepts optional date range parameters (from, to) and
Daily / weekly / monthly food cost % per venue. Trend line. Period-over-period comparison.
Theoretical vs actual usage per ingredient. Waste % over time. Flags anomalies.
All recipes sorted by gross margin %. Highlights underperformers.
Requires sales data. Shows food cost % relative to revenue. Period selectable.
Unit cost over time per ingredient / supplier. Surfaces price creep.
All POs with totals, lead times, and delivery performance per supplier.
per ingredient. Total inventory value at a point in time.
Small exports (< 5,000 rows): synchronous streaming CSV response.
. User receives an email with a pre-signed download link when the file is ready.
All exports are scoped to the active venue and authenticated org. Download links are pre-signed with a 24-hour expiry.
Set grace period flag (3 days). Notify org owner by email.
Downgrade org to Free plan immediately.
All Stripe webhook requests are verified with

using the endpoint's signing secret before any processing occurs. Invalid signatures return 400 and are logged. The Stripe Billing Portal is used for self-serve plan changes — no custom billing UI is required.
Business-tier organisations can register webhook endpoints to receive real-time events from
. This enables integration with POS systems, accounting software, and internal tooling.
contributing to a recipe is updated — exclusively triggered by a confirmed electronic invoice import.
An inventory movement brings stock below the
On event: Go handler enqueues an
with the payload and target endpoint IDs.
Worker executes the HTTP POST with a maximum 10-second timeout.
Payload is signed with HMAC-SHA256 using the endpoint's secret. Signature delivered in X-
Retry schedule: 3 attempts with exponential backoff — 1 minute, 5 minutes, 30 minutes.
Every attempt is logged to the
After 3 failed attempts, the endpoint is marked inactive and the org owner is notified.
The electronic invoice import pipeline is the single and exclusive way to update purchase prices and increment inventory stock. Suppliers upload their invoices in a standard electronic format (
for Italian suppliers, UBL or CII for international), and the system parses, matches, and confirms the data automatically.

update can occur without a parsed and user-confirmed electronic invoice line. This makes every stock entry and every price change traceable to a real fiscal document. Manual price overrides are still possible (
= manual) but do not create inventory movements.
Italian standard e-invoice. Used for all Italian suppliers. Parsed via Go's encoding/xml. Fields mapped:
(lines: quantity, unit price, description, tax rate).
Universal Business Language. Common in EU cross-border trade. Same parsing pipeline, different XPath mappings.
EN 16931 compliant format. Supported for completeness.
User uploads one or more invoice XML files via POST /venues
/invoices (multipart, max 10 MB per file, max 20 files per request).
API validates file size and MIME type (application/xml or text/xml). Returns 202 Accepted with a list of
is enqueued per file. Worker detects format (
vs UBL vs CII by root element), parses XML into typed structs, extracts all line items, and persists
looks up Supplier by SDI code (
) or VAT number. If matched, sets
. If no supplier found, the invoice is flagged for manual supplier assignment.
finds open POs for the matched supplier with invoice date within ±7 days of PO
. If found with high confidence, links invoice to PO (
User receives a notification (email + in-app) that invoices are ready for review.
After parsing, the user is presented with a matching review screen for each invoice. This is the only interactive step before stock and costs are updated.
Each parsed line shows: description from XML, quantity, unit price, tax rate. Confidence score shown as a colour indicator (green ≥90%, amber 60–89%, red <60%).
distance + token overlap against the venue's ingredient catalogue). User can override the match or create a new ingredient inline.
Shows the stock delta that will be applied to inventory. Unit conversion is offered if the invoice unit differs from the ingredient's canonical unit.
/invoices/:id/confirm. Locked until all lines are either matched or explicitly skipped.
Allows the user to exclude a line from import (e.g. non-food items like packaging). Skipped lines generate no movement and no cost update.
API validates all lines are matched or skipped. Returns 422 if any unresolved lines remain.
with the confirmed mapping. Returns 202 Accepted.
Worker runs the full confirmation pipeline inside a single Postgres transaction:
via EWMA for each matched ingredient.
If linked to a PO: advances PO to matched, then to closed.
notifies user by email with a summary (N ingredients updated, N movements created, total invoice value, any skipped lines).
On transaction failure: status set to error, error detail stored in
All raw XML is stored in
— a permanent link from a stock change to the exact invoice line that caused it.
The frontend invoice history page allows filtering by supplier, date range, and status. Each invoice can be expanded to show all lines, their matched ingredients, and the resulting movements.
Analytics report: Supplier price drift now sources exclusively from invoice-confirmed
records, giving exact per-delivery price history.
Full original XML as uploaded. Never modified.
| UBL | CII — detected at parse time.
Extracted from XML. Unique per supplier (enforced as unique constraint on
pending → parsed → matched (PO linked) → imported | error
. Null if no PO was matched.
Overall matching confidence (0–1) computed after ingredient matching. Stored for analytics.

CSV import is used for master data and manual stock counts. It does not create purchase movements or update ingredient costs. Those operations are exclusively handled by the electronic invoice pipeline (section 4.10).
is NOT a valid import column — prices come from electronic invoices only.
. Creates movements of type adjustment, not purchase.
API validates file size (max 50 MB) and MIME type, then enqueues
Worker processes rows in batches of 500. Each row is validated individually.
Errors are collected per-row and written to
(JSONB). The import does not stop on row errors.
On completion, the job status is
and the user is notified by email with a summary (N rows imported, M errors).

Template CSV files for each importable type are served as static assets from the Next.js frontend at /templates/*.csv. These are versioned and must be kept in sync with the import worker's column parser. The PO lines import type has been removed — PO line costs are sourced exclusively from electronic invoices.
Server components for fast TTFB, client islands for charts and interactivity
Zero external dependencies, explicit routing, fast binary startup
RLS for tenant isolation, JSONB for flexible fields, strong aggregation for analytics
Reliable task queue with retry, scheduling, and dead-letter support
Automatic TLS via Let's Encrypt, Docker label-based routing
Embedded in Go binary, runs at startup, version-controlled
Billing Portal for self-serve, webhooks for lifecycle events
Works with RSC/client boundary, no heavy dependencies
only for cross-island UI state (venue switcher, etc.)
for all services, no Kubernetes complexity at v1
/ UBL / CII XML parsed with zero external dependencies. Format detection by root element.
Ingredient matching heuristic for invoice lines. No ML dependency.
Ports 80/443. Docker provider enabled. Let's Encrypt ACME resolver. Dashboard disabled in prod.
Dev profile only. Port 8081. Connects to Redis.
The Go binary accepts a -mode flag at startup. Both the API server and the worker are compiled into the same binary and share all domain packages:
Starts the net/http server on :8080. Registers all route handlers. Connects to Postgres and Redis. Runs
server. Registers all task handlers. Connects to Postgres (for write-backs) and Redis (queue broker). No HTTP server.
All secrets are passed via environment variables, loaded from a .env file (never committed to version control).
Postgres and Redis use named volumes (
file documents every required variable with placeholder values and is
In production, secrets should be injected via the host environment or a secrets manager (e.g. Docker Secrets, AWS SSM).
approach is for local development only.

The Go codebase follows a strict layered architecture. Dependency arrows point inward only: handlers depend on domain, domain depends on nothing outside the standard library, stores implement domain interfaces.
— starts HTTP server, wires dependencies, runs migrations.
Pure business logic for recipe costing. No HTTP, no DB imports. Easily
Movement log logic, theoretical vs actual variance computation.
PO lifecycle state machine, EWMA cost update logic.
Menu engineering matrix classification, pricing suggestion formulas.
Report query builders (return typed structs, not raw SQL).
Plan tier definitions, feature flag map, Stripe event handlers.
Electronic invoice domain: XML format detection,
+ token overlap), EWMA cost update logic, confirmation transaction orchestrator.
login/register, magic link generation/verification, OAuth callbacks, JWT issuance, session management, refresh token lifecycle, password reset/change.
HTTP handlers. Validate request → call domain → call store → write response. Thin layer.
JWT validation, tenant extraction (sets PG session var), role check,
PostgreSQL repositories, one per domain. Implements interfaces defined in domain packages.
task type definitions, enqueuer helpers. Called by handlers and domain services.
task handlers. Calls domain and store packages. No HTTP context.
Redis client init. Session store: create/read/revoke/slide sessions. One-time token store: magic link, email verification, password reset tokens.
Stripe client, webhook signature verification, event dispatcher.
Resend/SES client wrapper. Called only from worker tasks.
Every HTTP handler follows the same structure to keep the codebase consistent and easy to navigate. Business logic is never in the handler layer:
Decode and validate the request body using a typed input struct.
Call the relevant domain function with typed parameters. Domain function returns a typed result or a domain error.
If the operation requires a database side effect, call the store repository.
Map domain errors to HTTP status codes (e.g.
Write the JSON response with encoding/
Email + password login form with a 'Forgot password' link and a 'Send magic link instead' toggle. Minimal UI, no layout chrome.
Token exchange. Calls Go API, sets
OAuth return handler. Same post-auth flow as magic link.
KPI summary cards (food cost %, top recipe by cost, low stock count) + food cost % trend chart.
Recipe list with cost and margin columns. Create/edit modal with ingredient builder and live cost preview.
Per-ingredient stock levels, movement history drawer, low-stock highlights, count entry form.
Supplier list. PO management: create, edit, track status, receive lines individually.
Menu engineering scatter matrix (Recharts). Item list with current vs suggested price delta.
Date-range picker, report selector, chart display, export button (triggers async export flow for large sets).
Org name, venue list (create/rename/archive), member management (invite, role change, remove).
Stripe Billing Portal embedded. Current plan display. Upgrade/downgrade CTA.
Register endpoint URLs, configure event filters, view delivery log per endpoint.
Electronic invoice upload page. Drag-and-drop XML upload, parsing progress, ingredient matching review screen per
invoice, confirm button. Invoice history list with status badges and audit detail.
CSV upload wizard for master data and manual stock counts (ingredients, recipes, portions sold). Template download links. Import job status polling.
React Server Components for data fetching
Data fetches happen on the server — no client-side loading spinners for initial page load. Reduces JS bundle sent to client.
Recipe ingredient builder, analytics charts, menu matrix, and the venue switcher are client components. Everything else is server-rendered.
The active venue ID must be shared across multiple client islands.
is used only for this — no global server state in client memory.
file checks for a valid session cookie on all /(app)/* routes. Redirects unauthenticated users to /login before any server component runs.
Stripe Billing Portal handles all plan changes, payment method updates, and invoice history. Zero billing UI code to maintain.

is used for five categories of work that must not block HTTP request handlers. The worker process runs as a separate container (same binary, -mode worker flag) and connects to the Redis queue broker and PostgreSQL for write-backs.
Sends via Resend/SES. Retries 3× with 1m/5m/15m backoff. Never called directly from HTTP handlers.
Renders and dispatches magic link email via
Renders and dispatches email address verification email.
Renders and dispatches password reset email.
POSTs JSON payload to registered endpoint with HMAC-SHA256 signature. Exponential backoff. Logs every attempt to
/UBL/CII), parses all lines, attempts supplier and PO auto-match, persists
+ lines with status = parsed. Notifies user when ready.
Runs inside a single Postgres transaction:
creates purchase movements, updates unit costs via EWMA, writes
, advances invoice + PO status. Notifies user on completion or failure.
Processes uploaded CSV in batches of 500 rows (master data and manual counts only — no purchase movements). Validates each row. Writes errors to
Streams query results to a temporary file. Generates a pre-signed download URL. Dispatches
Cron-style task scheduled by the worker. Finds all ingredients below
The security model applies controls at multiple layers so that a vulnerability in any single layer does not result in tenant data exposure.
PostgreSQL RLS policies enforced on every table. The Go API sets SET LOCAL
= $1 inside every transaction. A misconfigured handler cannot read another tenant's data.

Only the SHA-256 hash is stored in Redis. The raw token exists only in the email URL or cookie. All one-time tokens (magic link, email verification, password reset) are single-use and deleted from Redis immediately upon successful use.
=Strict cookies. SHA-256 hash stored in PostgreSQL
table (not Redis) for durability and auditability. Raw token exists only in the client cookie. Refresh tokens are rotated on every use.
All inbound Stripe events verified with
. Invalid signatures return 400 and are logged before any processing.
Every webhook payload signed with HMAC-SHA256 using the endpoint's unique secret. Receiving systems can verify integrity.
cost factor 12. The raw password is never stored or logged. The hash is never returned in any API response.
named parameters. No raw SQL string construction anywhere in the codebase.
All request bodies validated in Go using typed input structs before reaching domain or store layers. Invalid inputs return 422 with field-level error detail.
Files size-limited to 50 MB. MIME type validated server-side. Processing is always asynchronous — a malformed file cannot affect the HTTP server's availability.
Secrets injected via environment variables. Never committed to version control.
documents all variables with placeholder values only.

The following three architectural decisions cannot be made without additional input. They each have schema or API design implications that will affect multiple modules if changed after development begins.
Decision A — Popularity Data Source for Menu Engineering
The menu engineering matrix's popularity axis requires
field can be populated by: (1) manual entry per item per period, (2) CSV import on the existing import flow, or (3) a future POS webhook integration. The choice affects whether we need a
entity, how we handle time periods for the matrix, and whether the matrix is a point-in-time snapshot or a rolling window.
Decision B — Free Tier Plan Gate Behaviour
When a Free-tier user attempts to exceed their plan limits (e.g. adding a second venue, or creating recipe #51), the system can

(A) Hard block — return 403 Forbidden with an upgrade prompt. The venue/recipe is never created. (B) Soft lock — create the record but disable access/display until the user upgrades. Option A is simpler to implement and reason about. Option B allows a smoother trial experience. This decision affects the
middleware design and the frontend upgrade flow.

Sub-recipes (a recipe that uses another recipe as an ingredient) require recursive cost rollup. The current design proposes a practical cap of 3 levels to keep query complexity manageable. If unlimited depth is required, the Go domain layer needs cycle detection (DFS with a visited set) and the Postgres cost query
a recursive CTE. Confirm the maximum nesting level before implementing the
HMAC-SHA256 signing secret for access token JWTs. Minimum 32 random bytes.
cost factor for password hashing. Default: 12. Lower only in test environments.
Max size per uploaded invoice XML file in megabytes. Default: 10.
Max number of invoice files per upload request. Default: 20.
Alpha factor for EWMA ingredient cost updates. Default: 0.3. Range: 0.0–1.0.
Separate secret for refresh token HMAC. Must differ from JWT_SECRET.
Resend API key for transactional email.
From address for all outbound emails. e.g. noreply@foodcost.app
Public base URL. Used to construct magic link and download link URLs.
development | staging | production. Controls logging level and error verbosity.
Register with email + password. Sends verification email.
Login with email + password. Returns JWT + sets refresh cookie.
Exchange magic link or email verification token.
Reset password using token from email. Revokes all sessions.
Change password (requires current password). Revokes all other sessions.
Handle Google OAuth callback. Issues session.
Rotate refresh token. Returns new JWT + new refresh cookie.
Revoke current session and refresh token.
Revoke all sessions and refresh tokens for the user.
List organisations the authenticated user belongs to
Invite a user to the organisation
Remove a member from the organisation
Switch active venue — reissues JWT with new
Upload one or more electronic invoice XML files. Returns
List all invoices with status, supplier, date, total amount.
Get parsed invoice detail: header, all lines with matching status and confidence score.
List or create recipes with computed cost
Get, update, or archive a recipe
List ingredients with current unit cost
Record receipt for PO lines (triggers inventory + cost update)
List menu items with pricing suggestions
